source.title: Molecular residual disease and efficacy of adjuvant chemotherapy in patients with colorectal cancer.

finding.description: Postsurgical ctDNA positivity at 4 weeks after surgery was associated with higher recurrence risk in patients with stage II or III CRC.
finding.effect_summary: Higher recurrence risk with postsurgical ctDNA positivity.

source_quote: In this cohort, with a median follow-up of 16.74 months (range 0.49-24.83 months), postsurgical ctDNA positivity (at 4 weeks after surgery) was associated with higher recurrence risk (hazard ratio (HR) 10.0, P < 0.0001) and was the most significant prognostic factor associated with recurrence risk in patients with stage II or III CRC (HR 10.82, P < 0.001).

source.title: Molecular residual disease and efficacy of adjuvant chemotherapy in patients with colorectal cancer.

finding.description: Patients with stage II or III CRC and postsurgical ctDNA positivity derived benefit from ACT.
finding.effect_summary: Benefit from ACT in ctDNA-positive patients.

source_quote: Furthermore, postsurgical ctDNA positivity identified patients with stage II or III CRC who derived benefit from ACT (HR 6.59, P < 0.0001).

source.title: Molecular residual disease and efficacy of adjuvant chemotherapy in patients with colorectal cancer.

finding.description: 18-month DFS was 38.4% for ctDNA-positive patients versus 90.5% for ctDNA-negative patients.

source_quote: Among 187 patients who were ctDNA positive 4 weeks after surgery, 61.4% (115 out of 187) experienced recurrence, whereas only 9.5% (81 out of 852) of patients who were ctDNA negative 4 weeks after surgery experienced recurrence (hazard ratio (HR) 10.0, 95% confidence interval (CI) 7.7–14.0, P < 0.0001), demonstrating an 18-month DFS of 38.4% (95% CI 31.4–45.5%) versus 90.5% (95% CI 88.3–92.3%), respectively.

source.title: Molecular residual disease and efficacy of adjuvant chemotherapy in patients with colorectal cancer.

finding.description: No significant benefit from ACT in ctDNA-negative patients.
finding.effect_summary: No significant benefit from ACT in ctDNA-negative patients.

source_quote: In ctDNA-negative patients, after accounting for potentially confounding factors (age, sex, pathological stage, MSI and performance status), no statistically significant benefit from ACT was observed (adjusted HR 1.71, 95% CI 0.80–3.7, P = 0.167).

source.title: Molecular residual disease and efficacy of adjuvant chemotherapy in patients with colorectal cancer.

finding.description: ACT was associated with a higher cumulative incidence of ctDNA clearance.

source_quote: In this analysis, ACT was associated with a higher estimated cumulative incidence of ctDNA clearance in 68.48% (63 out of 92) of patients by week 24 after surgery compared with 12.2% (11 out of 90) of patients who did not receive ACT (adjusted HR 8.50, 95% CI 4.2–17.3, P < 0.0001; HR was adjusted for sex, age, stage, MSI and performance status).

source.title: Molecular residual disease and efficacy of adjuvant chemotherapy in patients with colorectal cancer.

finding.description: Patients who converted from ctDNA negative to positive had a significantly higher risk of recurrence.
finding.effect_summary: Higher recurrence risk for patients converting from ctDNA negative to positive.

source_quote: Compared with the risk of recurrence for patients who were persistently negative, a significantly higher risk of recurrence was observed for patients who converted from ctDNA negative to positive (HR 14.0, 95% CI 8.5–24.0, P < 0.001), with an 18-month DFS of 33.8% (95% CI 18.1–50.2%).

source.title: Molecular residual disease and efficacy of adjuvant chemotherapy in patients with colorectal cancer.

finding.description: The American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) panel states that the current definition of ‘high-risk’ stage II cancer is inadequate.

source_quote: The American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) panel states that the current definition of ‘high-risk’ stage II cancer is inadequate as many patients with high-risk features do not experience recurrence, whereas some patients with average-risk features do.
